title: World History
whatItIs:
World history is the study of human civilisation across its full sweep — from the emergence of Homo sapiens in Africa roughly 300,000 years ago, through the agricultural revolution, the rise and fall of empires, the transformations of the medieval and early modern periods, the Industrial Revolution, the upheavals of the twentieth century, and the globalised present. Unlike national histories that focus on one country's narrative, world history examines the connections, exchanges, conflicts, and parallel developments that shaped humanity as a whole. It asks not just what happened, but why civilisations rose and fell, how ideas and technologies spread across continents, and whose stories have been told — and whose have been erased.

The great ages of human history each transformed the species in fundamental ways. The Neolithic Revolution (roughly 10,000 BCE) turned nomadic hunter-gatherers into settled agriculturalists, enabling population growth, social stratification, and the first cities. The Bronze Age saw the rise of Mesopotamia, Egypt, the Indus Valley, and Shang China. The Iron Age brought the Greek polis, the Roman Republic, the Maurya Empire, and the philosophical traditions that still underpin modern thought. The medieval period was not a 'dark age' but a time of extraordinary innovation in the Islamic Golden Age, Song Dynasty China, and medieval Europe. The Renaissance, Enlightenment, and Industrial Revolution then set the stage for European colonialism, the modern nation-state, and the world wars that reshaped the global order.

Critically, world history must grapple with perspective. The version of history taught in most Western schools centres European agency and treats other civilisations as backdrop. A genuine world history gives equal weight to the Mongol Empire (which connected Eurasia through trade, technology transfer, and cultural exchange on a scale Europe would not match for centuries), to the maritime networks of the Indian Ocean, to pre-Columbian civilisations in the Americas, and to the African kingdoms that European colonisers systematically erased from the historical record. Real history includes the stories of winners and losers, colonisers and colonised, rulers and the ruled.
whyItMatters:
Understanding history is not about memorising dates — it is about recognising patterns. Why do empires rise and fall? What causes revolutions? How does technology reshape power structures? Why do societies repeat mistakes that seem obvious in hindsight? The patterns of history illuminate the present: you cannot understand modern geopolitics without understanding colonialism, you cannot understand the Middle East without understanding the Ottoman Empire and the Sykes-Picot Agreement, and you cannot understand economic inequality without understanding the centuries of wealth extraction that created it.

History also teaches epistemic humility. Every era believed itself to be the pinnacle of progress, and every era was wrong about things it was certain about. The Romans were confident their civilisation would last forever. Victorian Europeans were certain that their racial hierarchy reflected natural law. Understanding how completely and confidently past societies were mistaken should make us more careful about our own certainties. Perhaps most importantly, history gives voice to the dead — it is the only discipline that attempts to understand the full scope of human experience across time, including the experiences of those who lost, who were conquered, who were enslaved, and whose stories the victors tried to silence.
keyIdeas: The Neolithic Revolution and the birth of agriculture and civilisation
Ancient civilisations — Mesopotamia, Egypt, Indus Valley, China, Mesoamerica
The Bronze Age, Iron Age, and classical antiquity
The medieval world — Islamic Golden Age, Song Dynasty, European feudalism
The Industrial Revolution and its global consequences
Colonialism, imperialism, and the reshaping of the non-European world
Twentieth-century wars, decolonisation, and the emergence of the modern global order
frontier: Digital humanities and computational history are opening new ways to study the past — analysing millions of historical documents with natural language processing, reconstructing ancient trade networks through isotope analysis of artefacts, and using ancient DNA to rewrite migration histories that were previously based on speculation. Archaeological advances continue to push back dates and overturn assumptions: Gobekli Tepe revealed monumental architecture thousands of years before agriculture was supposed to have begun. Revisionist historiography is also gaining ground, with scholars from formerly colonised nations rewriting histories that were previously told only from the coloniser's perspective, recovering indigenous knowledge systems, and challenging Eurocentric periodisation frameworks.
openQuestions: Is there a pattern to the rise and fall of civilisations, or is each case unique?
How much of recorded history reflects what actually happened versus what the powerful wanted remembered?
Can understanding historical patterns help us predict or prevent future conflicts?
How should history be taught to include the perspectives of the conquered and colonised alongside the conquerors?
What major historical events or civilisations remain poorly understood due to lack of surviving records?
Is the modern nation-state a permanent feature of human organisation, or a temporary phase like the city-state or empire before it?
